The 95% Accuracy Illusion

Your model achieved 95% accuracy in the lab. You deployed it with confidence. Six months later, it's barely better than random guessing. What happened?

Our Agenda: A Framework for Resilience

Today's Mission: From Fragile Models to Resilient Systems

The Core Modes: A dive into why models break

The Framework: to build your instrument panel

The Remediation Playbook: to fix what's broken

The Principles of Production ML: Best practices for success

The Core Failure Modes

- Data Drift (Covariate Shift)
- Label Drift (Prior Probability Shift)
- Concept Drift
- Feature Pipeline Degradation & Train-Serve Skew
- Feedback Loops & Bias Amplification

Failure Mode 1: Data Drift

The Zillow Buying Failure: In 2021, Zillow's automated home-buying division, Zillow Offers, collapsed, leading to a staggering $881 million loss and the layoff of 25% of its workforce. While multiple factors were at play, a core issue was the failure of their pricing models to adapt to a rapidly changing housing market. The models, trained on historical data, were unprepared for the unprecedented market volatility and shifting buyer preferences of the post-pandemic era - a classic, and costly, example of data drift.

Data Drift:
Detection

Data drift occurs when the distribution of the input data in production, P_prod(X), diverges significantly from the distribution of the training data, P_train(X).

We can quantify this divergence using statistical tests:

- Population Stability Index (PSI)
- Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test
- Jensen-Shannon (J-S) Divergence

Population Stability Index (PSI)

Measures the change in the distribution of a single variable. It is calculated by binning the data and comparing the percentage of observations in each bin between the two samples.

Excellent for tracking changes in key features over time. A common rule of thumb is that a PSI > 0.25 indicates significant drift.

To calculate the PSI we first divide the initial population range into 10 buckets (an arbitrary number I chose), and count the number of values in each of those buckets for the initial and new populations, and then divide those by the total values in each population to get the percents in each bucket

From here, we perform the actual PSI calculation for each bucket, and them sum them all up to get the overall PSI values for the distributions.

Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test

A non-parametric test that compares the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of two samples. It is sensitive to differences in both location and shape of the distributions.

Best for continuous numerical features. The test returns a p-value; a low p-value (e.g., < 0.05) suggests the distributions are different.

The data you collect for a variable or feature in either training or production may look like the distributions below. An empirical CDF is created by ordering the data by value and creating a piecewise graph.

Jensen-Shannon (J-S) Divergence

Measures the similarity between two probability distributions. It is a symmetrized and smoothed version of the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence.

Useful for comparing entire probability distributions, especially for categorical features.

Between two distributions P, Q of a continuous feature Kullback-Leibler divergence is defined as:

D_KL(P||Q) = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} p(x) ln(p(x)/q(x)) dx

where p(x) and q(x) are the probability density functions of the distributions P, Q respectively. And Jensen-Shannon Divergence is defined as:

D_JS(P||Q) = 1/2 [D_KL(P || 1/2(P+Q)) + D_KL(Q || 1/2(P+Q))]

Failure Mode 2: Concept Drift

The Ever-Evolving World of Fraud: Fraud detection models are in a constant battle against concept drift. A model trained to detect fraudulent credit card transactions might learn that transactions from a certain country late at night are high-risk. Fraudsters adapt. They might start using VPNs to appear as if they are in a low-risk country or switch their attack times. The features of the transaction (amount, location, time) might look the same, but their relationship to the likelihood of fraud has changed because the adversary’s strategy has evolved. This is a form of adversarial adaptation, a particularly aggressive type of concept drift.

Concept Drift: Detection

Technical Deep Dive & Detection: Concept drift is often more challenging to detect than data drift because it requires ground truth labels (Y) to observe the changing relationship. You can’t spot it just by looking at the input data (X). The primary indicator is a degradation in model performance (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) over time, even when the input data distribution remains stable.

Detection Methods:

- Performance Monitoring
- Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN)
- Drift Detection Method (DDM)

Performance Monitoring

The most direct method. Continuously track key model metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, AUC) over time on a labeled validation set. A sustained drop is a strong indicator of concept drift.

This should be the first line of defense for any production model.

Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN)

An algorithm that maintains a sliding window of recent data. It automatically grows or shrinks the window size when the statistical properties of the data within the window change, signaling a drift.

Effective for detecting both sudden and gradual drifts in streaming data environments.

Drift Detection Method (DDM)

Monitors the model’s error rate. It models the error rate as a binomial distribution and triggers a warning when the error rate exceeds a certain threshold, suggesting the underlying concept has changed.

Well-suited for classification tasks where you can track the error rate in near real-time.

Failure Mode 3: Label Drift

Economic Recessions and Loan Defaults: A model built to predict loan defaults is trained during a period of economic stability, where the default rate is a low and stable 2%. An unexpected recession hits, and unemployment skyrockets. Suddenly, the actual default rate in the population jumps to 10%. The model, calibrated on the 2% rate, will now severely underestimate the risk across the board, potentially leading to massive financial losses for the lending institution. Its internal assumptions about the base rate of default are no longer valid.

Label Drift: Detection

Technical Deep Dive & Detection: Label drift is detected by monitoring the distribution of the target variable itself. Unlike concept drift, you don't necessarily need to evaluate the entire model's performance to spot it, but you do need access to ground truth labels over time.

Detection methods:

- Monitor Class Distribution
- Chi-Square Test
- Track Prediction Distribution

Monitor Class Distribution

The most straightforward method. Track the proportion of each class in your labeled data over time. A significant change in these proportions is a direct indication of label drift.

Essential for any classification task. Can be visualized with a simple time series plot of class percentages.

Chi-Square Test

A statistical test that can be used to determine if there is a significant association between two categorical variables. In this case, you can compare the distribution of labels in a reference window to the distribution in a current window.

Good for formally testing if the change in class distribution is statistically significant.

Chi-squared distribution — shaded area = rejection region (α = 0.05, df = 3)

χ² statistic: 0.00
critical value: 7.78
p-value: 1.000
df: 3

No significant drift — χ²=0.00 < critical 7.78, p=1.000.

Chi-squared distribution — shaded area = rejection region (α = 0.05, df = 3)

χ² statistic: 9.63
critical value: 7.78
p-value: 0.022
df: 3

Drift detected — χ²=9.63 > critical 7.78, p=0.022. Trigger alert.

Track Prediction Distribution

In the absence of immediate ground truth, you can monitor the distribution of your model’s predictions. If a model that used to predict 5% positive cases suddenly starts predicting 15%, it could be a (noisy) indicator of label drift.

Useful as an early warning system when ground truth labels are delayed. However, it can be confounded by data drift, so it should be used with caution.

Label Drift: Resolution

- Recalibration: The model's outputs can often be adjusted post-hoc to account for the new class distribution. Techniques like Platt Scaling or Isotonic Regression can be used to recalibrate the model's probability scores to better match the new reality.
- Importance Weighting: During retraining, you can assign weights to the training samples to make the training data distribution resemble the production data distribution. If the proportion of class 'A' has doubled in production, you can give a higher weight to samples of class 'A' during training.
- Cost-Sensitive Learning: If the cost of misclassifying different classes is different (e.g., failing to detect a fraudulent transaction is much worse than flagging a legitimate one), you can adjust the learning algorithm to penalize the more costly errors more heavily. This can make the model more robust to changes in class distribution.

Failure Mode 4: Feature Pipeline Degradation & Train-Serve Skew

Google’s Diabetic Retinopathy AI in the Real World:
Google developed a state-of-the-art deep learning model that could detect diabetic retinopathy from retinal scans with accuracy on par with human ophthalmologists. In the lab, using high-quality, perfectly captured images, it was a resounding success. However, when deployed in real-world clinics in Thailand, its performance stumbled. The nurses, working in busy, poorly-lit conditions, often captured images that were of lower quality than the pristine training data. The system had a high rejection rate for these real-world images, and intermittent internet connectivity hampered the cloud-based analysis. This is a classic case of train-serve skew: the features (retinal images) available at prediction time were systematically different from the features used to train the model, not because of a change in the patient's eyes, but because of the data capture process itself.

Feature Pipeline Degradation: Detection

Issue Type                     Detection Method

Data Quality Issues            Data Validation Frameworks that allow you to define a suite of expectations for your data (e.g., expect_column_values_to_not_be_null, expect_column_mean_to_be_between). These checks should run every time new data is ingested.

Schema Changes                Schema Monitoring. Your pipeline should automatically ingest and validate the schema of incoming data against a known-good reference schema. Alerts should be triggered on any discrepancy.

Train-Serve Skew              Prediction Validation & Shadow Deployment. Log the inputs and outputs of both the training and production environments for a sample of data. If f_train(X) and f_prod(X) produce different results for the same input X, you have a skew. Shadowing (running the new model alongside the old one without acting on its predictions) is a powerful way to detect this before full deployment.

Data Quality Issues

The data itself is corrupted. This includes a sudden increase in NULL values, the appearance of outliers, or incorrect data types (e.g., a string appearing in a numeric column).

> Python

expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("user_id")
expect_column_values_to_be_between("age", 0, 120)
expect_column_values_to_be_unique("transaction_id")

Schema Changes

An upstream data source changes its structure. A column is renamed, removed, or its data type is changed. This will often cause the feature pipeline to crash entirely.

The upstream team ships a database migration. Three breaking changes land silently — no versioning, no announcement.

Upstream DB  -->  Ingestion  -->  Feature eng.  -->  Feature store  -->  Model

Before (original)

revenue          FLOAT
signup_date      STRING
is_active        BOOL

After (migrated)

signup_date      type changed  INT
is_active        BOOL

country          removed

Train-Serve Skew

The logic used to create features for training is different from the logic used in production. This can be subtle, such as using a library with a different version that has a minor implementation change, or it can be blatant, like using data that would not actually be available at prediction time.

TRAIN-SERVE SKEW
Different logic for feature pipeline leads to different outcomes

TRAINING PIPELINE
Raw Data -> Feature Logic (FRY) -> Training Features
"I FRY THE FISH!"
TRAINING MEAL (FRIED FISH)

PRODUCTION PIPELINE
Raw Data -> Feature Logic (BOIL) -> Serving Features
"I BOIL THE FISH!"
PRODUCTION MEAL (BOILED FISH)

WHY ARE OUR MEALS SO DIFFERENT?

DIFFERENT FEATURES -> DIFFERENT MODEL BEHAVIOR

Failure Mode 5: Feedback Loops & Bias Amplification

The COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm: The COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) tool has been used across the U.S. justice system to predict the likelihood of a defendant re-offending. A landmark 2016 investigation by ProPublica revealed that the algorithm was significantly biased against Black defendants, flagging them as high-risk for future crimes at nearly twice the rate as white defendants with similar histories. This creates a pernicious feedback loop. A defendant flagged as high-risk is more likely to receive a longer sentence, be denied parole, and face heavier policing, all of which can increase the likelihood of a future arrest, regardless of their individual actions. The model's prediction becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy, and this biased outcome is then recorded as a "correct" data point, further training the model to perpetuate the same bias.

Feedback Loops & Bias Amplification: Detection

Technical Deep Dive & Detection: Feedback loops occur when the model's output, ŷ_t,
influences the state of the world and thus the future data, X_t+1, that the model will be trained
on. This can lead to a gradual narrowing of the data distribution, E[X_t], towards a biased
subset over time, amplifying any initial biases in the model or data.

Detection methods:

- Diversity & Exposure Metrics
- A/B Testing with a Control Group
- Causal Inference Techniques

Diversity & Exposure Metrics

Quantify the variety of predictions the model is making. For a recommendation system, this could be the number of unique items recommended over a period. For a loan application model, it could be the demographic distribution of approved applicants. A sharp decrease in diversity is a red flag.

Crucial for recommendation systems, content feeds, and any system that personalizes user experience.

A/B Testing with a Control Group

Run a small percentage of users through a "random" or non-personalized version of the model. Compare the behavior and outcomes of this control group to the main group. If the model-driven group’s behavior diverges significantly over time, a strong feedback loop is likely present.

The gold standard for identifying the causal impact of a model on user behavior.

Causal Inference Techniques

Employ more advanced statistical methods to try and disentangle the model's influence from the user's organic preferences. Techniques like uplift modeling can help estimate the true persuasive effect of a prediction.

Uplift modeling in recommendation systems uses causal inference to estimate the incremental impact of an intervention (e.g., a recommendation, coupon, or discount) on user behavior, distinguishing users who only act because of the recommendation from those who would act anyway or not at all.

Feedback Loops & Bias Amplification: Resolution

- Exploration Strategies: Intentionally introduce randomness into the model's predictions to break the feedback loop. In a recommendation system, this could mean occasionally showing items that the model is uncertain about or that are outside the user's typical interest profile (an ε-greedy approach). This allows the model to collect data on a wider range of possibilities.
- Positional Debiasing: In ranked lists (like search results or recommendations), users are more likely to click on items at the top. The model can misinterpret this as a stronger preference. Positional debiasing techniques adjust for this by explicitly modeling the probability of a click given its position in the list.
- Fairness Constraints: During model training, you can introduce mathematical constraints to ensure that the model's predictions satisfy certain fairness criteria. For example, you could require that the rate of loan approvals be statistically similar across different demographic groups, a concept known as "demographic parity."

A Unified Monitoring Framework

The Three Pillars of Monitoring: A comprehensive framework rests on three pillars, each tracking a different aspect of your model’s operational reality.

Pillar              | What It Tracks                                      | Why It Matters                                                                                     | Key Metrics
-------------------|------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Model Performance   | The ultimate business value and predictive power of your model. | This is the ground truth of your model’s effectiveness. A decline here is a direct threat to the ROI of the project. | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC, Log-Loss, Business-specific KPIs (e.g., revenue per recommendation, click-through rate).
Data Health         | The statistical integrity of the data being fed to your model. | This is your early warning system. Data issues are the leading cause of performance degradation, and detecting them here allows you to react before business metrics are impacted. | PSI, K-S Statistic, Null Rates, Data Type & Schema Compliance, Outlier Rates, Feature Min/Max/Mean.
System Health       | The operational performance of the infrastructure serving the model. | A model that gives perfect predictions too slowly is useless. System health ensures your model is meeting its Service Level Agreements (SLAs). | Inference Latency (p50, p95, p99), Throughput (predictions per second), Error Rates (e.g., HTTP 500s), CPU/Memory/GPU Utilization.

The Best Practices: Principles of Production ML

Design for Drift from Day 1 - Build monitoring and retraining into your architecture upfront, not as an afterthought.

Implement Gradual Rollouts
→ Shadow → Canary → Full. Never deploy directly to 100% traffic.

Document Everything - Model Cards, Data Lineage, Decision Logs. Future you will thank you.

Maintain Training-Serving Parity - Use identical code, libraries, and data sources for both training and inference. (Feature stores are the gold standard.)

Instrument for Feedback - Collect ground truth on predictions. This fuels both monitoring and retraining.

Focus in Predictive AI

- Data foundation
  - We have massive CS2 data; .dem files can be parsed into time series for analysis.

- AI opportunities
  - Win predictions, game situation embeddings for vector search & ranking.
  - Kill/survival forecasts and impact situation detection

  - AWS solutions for F1 & Bundesliga inspire our approach.

Getting the data

- Local R&D
  - On-premise GPUs for non-CS2 research & prototyping.
  - Easier to start locally due to available HW and skills gap for cloud builds.

- Data Transfer Challenges
  - Moving ~100 TB from S3 was slower and costlier than expected (est. 15k USD).
  - Direct download not practical for large volumes.

- AWS Snowball Solution
  - Discovered Snowball as a cost-effective alternative for bulk transfer.
  - Plug & play on local network, supports AWS CLI & DataSync.
  - Learned some practical gotchas (e.g., configure NFS before ordering).

AWS Snowball

Data Transfer

Fast and secure migration of large datasets to AWS when network transfer isn't feasible.

From raw data to delta lake

1. Challenge
   - Raw .dem files not ready for ML training.
   - Our solution was built on-premise, but designed from the start for an easy migration to AWS once the R&D phase is complete.

2. Solution
   - Adopted Delta Lake as the central data store
   - Implemented Medallion architecture (Bronze / Silver / Gold)

3. Technical benefits
   - Parquet-based format -> columnar, high compression, compatible with Polars, Spark, Daft
   - Z-Ordering -> faster queries, crucial for PyTorch data loaders
     (Alternative: Liquid clustering – Spark only)

4. Outcome
   - Unified, efficient, and portable data environment ready for ML pipelines

From unstructured files to a scalable ML-ready data platform
   - We transformed raw .dem files into a Delta Lake architecture to create a scalable, high-performance foundation for machine learning.
   - This approach unifies data storage, accelerates queries, and prepares our solution for future migration to AWS.

Bronze tier data

Unaltered CS2 game demo telemetry
- One datapoint in CS2 is called tick = current game state
- We have 64 ticks in 1 second of the game

Tick-level data
- Each player has [X,Y,Z] coordinates for position together with velocity
- Team, health, armor, inventory, active weapon, yaw/pitch
- Individual buttons pressed
- Info about time in round, position of bomb, time to explosion

Event-level data
- Combat - player hurt/death, weapon fire - attacker/victim, weapon, position of event
- Bomb lifecycle - begin planting, bomb planted, begin defusing, bomb defused, bomb exploded
- "Meta events" - e.g., player disconnected

Silver/gold tier data

Moving from raw telemetry to consolidated data
- Data are cleaned, e.g., invalid or incomplete matches are dropped
- Added high-level summaries such as round winners, win reasons, and duration

Feature engineering
- Data are now in wide format (i.e., pivoted)
- Subsampled ticks - 32 Hz
- Distance matrix for player positions
- Feature engineering is done in gold layer - model specific
- Scaling is applied based on feature distribution
- Aggregations calculated - e.g., # of alive players, equip-value difference between teams, K/D/A from scoreboard, ...
- Labels are pre-computed
  - Will player damage anyone in next X seconds?, Where will player N move?, Round/Match result

General Pretrained Rankacy Transformer

Architecture
- Two model variants: Flash (static) and Pro (sequence-focused)
- 75M parameters, 8 encoder blocks, trained on 160M input instances
  - (Reference: Google BERT – 12 encoders, ~100M parameters)
- Hierarchical tokenization -> teams, alive/dead players

Inputs
- Dynamic: player positions, health, grenade explosions
- Static: kill/death ratios, past round outcomes

Outputs
- Round & match win prediction
- Micro-event forecasting: kills, survivals, bomb plants/defuses, player positions

Technical details of the GPRT Pro model

- 96 ticks – 3 seconds as an input sequence
- Trained on 2x A5000 – 20 days
- Computational speed was #1 priority – constant 90-100 % GPU usage
  - Optimized dataloaders
  - Batch diversity with MNML raw Parquets opened by Delta read operations
  - Gradient accumulation over multiple batches – more stable loss
  - Latest architecture perks - RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm – 10 % speedup in norm layers

- We run change-point detection algorithms over the probability curve to find impact moments

- This is a backbone for the round summaries and other features

GPRT Attention maps & debugging
GPRT Attention maps & debugging

Toughest Challenges
Performance, stability, and data processing

Model Export
- ONNX conversion still unreliable for complex transformer architectures
- Some layers and attention patterns not fully supported

Polars Instability
- Version inconsistency → performance can vary N-times
- Unstable query planner (sometimes eager > lazy)
- Memory leaks with thousands of Parquet files
- Non-persistent workers in PyTorch required for consistent speed

Integration Challenges
- delta-rs ↔ Polars ↔ Spark writer performance gaps
- Ongoing optimization of I/O throughput and memory management

Model deploy on AWS

Main System Components
- ML Processor – takes raw .dem files and runs ETL for bronze/silver/gold layer transform
  - Saves temporary Parquet files to S3
- Delta Lake Writer – writes temp. data in Parquet format to Delta lake
- Inference Gateway – runs GPRT Pro inference jobs
  - We run model inference on Hetzner GPU-powered VPS
  - Lower costs compared to AWS
    - $3000 on AWS vs. $300 on Hetzner for GPU
    - We had 2 instances on AWS - less powerful instances
    - On Hetzner we need just one for the same traffic
    - ...but we lost scaling capabilities on AWS
    - ...and there is an egress cost in AWS S3 -> Hetzner VPS

ML Processor Workflow
- Demo Processor
  - RabbitMQ (Input)
    - Amazon ElastiCache
  - ML Processor
    - Demo to Bronze
    - Bronze to Silver
    - Silver to Gold
  - Amazon S3 Parquet Temporary Data
  - RabbitMQ (Write)

ML Processor Writer
  - Delta Lake Writer
  - RabbitMQ (Inference)

Data Lake
  - Amazon EMR
  - Delta Lake
    - Bronze (6 TB)
    - Silver (6 TB)
    - Gold (VL 20 TB)
  - AWS Glue Data Catalog
  - Amazon S3
  - Vector Database

Interface
  - GPRT Atlas

  - GPRT Pro

Rankacy Platform
  - Amazon S3 Arrow files for FE

Prediction and data for LLM x AI

Model deploy on AWS

Inference Cost Optimization
- Utilizing Graviton (ARM) instances to achieve the best performance-per-dollar ratio in the cluster
- Parquet files stored for a rolling 3-day window
- Majority of active inference requests - low egress and latency

Efficient Data Persistence
- Delta Lake Writer operates with on-demand instance
- Single writer on powerful instance (r8g.8xlarge, 256GB, 32 CPUs)
- Batched writes – reduce write-frequency overhead
- All tables are structured using Z-Ordering to enable lightning-fast data skipping

Lifecycle & Maintenance
- Custom-built Spark environment designed with Labyrinth Labs
- Spark runs on EKS - Mainly Z-Ordering + VACUUM tasks

Model deploy on AWS

First deploy – $7-8k/month
- Every service on AWS - no Hetzner yet
- Experiments with Amazon EMR + instances for Spark on EKS
- Too big partitions in Delta lake for our traffic
- We have designed everything for half the traffic initially
- Platform grew very fast -> latency issues were common

Current deploy – $3-4k/month
- Mixed AWS/Hetzner solution
- Optimized data pipelines based on real-world usage data
- Selective data storing, different partitioning, non-managed services...
- 4-6x higher data throughput compared to previous deploy
- There is still room for optimization
- Mainly in S3 data storage lifecycle ($ vs. UX)
- Distillation of GPRT Pro, ONNX inference, ...

Current focus

Human behaviour modelling
- ORBIT - Optimized Response, Behavior, & Instinct Transformer
- Custom visibility engine for precise enemy-visibility estimates
- Predictions of limbic system reactions (sub-100 ms) for the players

Autonomous agents
- TITAN — Transformer Imitation for Tactical Agent Navigation
- Bots for CS2 environment - movement, actions in game
- Connected to ORBIT above - developed aimbot by mistake
- Goal is to transfer the knowledge beyond CS2

Production-ready solutions
- GPRT-like models for real-time CS2 streams
- Use-cases in e-sport and iGaming industry
- Tournament data overlays with predictions
- Round outcome, expected-kill modelling and many more

**Face recognition pipeline**

1. **Detection**
   Locate every face in the input image and provide a bbox and detection for the face.

2. **Keypoints detection**
   Find facial landmarks in the face. Used to align the face for downstream stages.

3. **Quality assessment**
   Filter out samples too poor to process — blur, resolution, etc.

4. **Liveness detection**
   Reject presentation attacks: masks, printed photos, replays.

5. **Representation extraction**
   Encode the aligned face into a fixed-size embedding vector.

6. **Matching**
   Compare two embeddings to produce a similarity score.

Data curation

A model trained with only 32% of a cleaned MegaFace subset, or 20% of a cleaned MS-Celeb-1M subset, matches the performance of models trained on the full (noisy) dataset.

Wang et al., "The Devil of Face Recognition is in the Noise", ECCV 2018

---
Fig. 7. 1:1M rank-1 identification results on MegaFace benchmark: (a) introducing label flips to IMDb-Face, (b) introducing outliers to IMDb-Face, and (c) fixing the size of clean data and dilute it with different ratios of label flips.

Data cleaning process

1. Deduplication & low-quality filtering
   - Remove exact/near-duplicates and samples too degraded to supervise training reliably.

2. Clustering
   - Group samples by identity. Skippable if reliable subject IDs are already provided.

3. Intra-class cleaning
   - Within each cluster: remove outliers, or split when a cluster captures two identities.

4. Inter-class cleaning
   - Across clusters: merge those that actually belong to the same identity.

Human in the loop

Ambiguous sample flagged by automated filters

Training data
-> Remove the uncertain sample
   Noisy labels hurt generalization more than they help. When the reviewer is not confident, the safest choice is to drop the sample — clean data wins over more data.

Evaluation data
-> Keep and label carefully
   Dropping uncertain samples removes exactly the hard cases the benchmark is meant to measure. The result is an optimistic metric that does not reflect real-world deployment.

Mitigation

Balancing data
Ensure proportional representation across demographics in training datasets

Fairness-aware training
Apply loss functions that penalize performance disparities between groups

Threshold calibration
Set demographic-specific thresholds to equalize FMR/FNMR across cohorts

Synthetic data
Use generative models to augment underrepresented demographic groups

### Conclusion

**WHAT WE COVERED**

1. **Data quality is crucial**
   Clean data wins — both for training and for evaluation. It is better to sacrifice some data, then have noise.

2. **Controlled scenarios has low error rate**
   Error rates are very low on cooperative captures. For identification, accuracy depends on FPIR and gallery size.

3. **There are still challenging cases**
   Long-range captures, low quality and low resolution faces, high pitch/yaw angles.

4. **Demographic bias matters**
   Measurable, hurts accuracy — field is paying more attention to that phenomenon.

**OPEN QUESTIONS**

? **Will synthetic data become a viable training source?**
   Generated faces sidestep privacy constraints and class imbalance — if quality and identity diversity hold up.

? **Will general-purpose models match or beat expert FR?**
   In five years, will anyone still be training a dedicated face recognition model — or will we just prompt a VLM?

Contrastive Forecasting: Latent-Space Prediction for Time Series via Joint Embedding

Jeremy Cochoy, Redstone Solutions

Contrastive Forecasting
Latent-Space Prediction for Time Series via Joint Embedding

Jeremy Cochoy
CEO, Redstone Solutions

Machine Learning Prague · 2026

The LLM recipe came to time series

Language model – next token
The river is wide and ?

Time-series foundation model – next patch
[graph images] [graph images] [graph images] [graph images] [graph images] ?

- Next-token prediction runs deeper than it looks: you need to encode grammar, style, world knowledge, arithmetic.
- Time-series foundation models follow the same recipe: patched inputs, transformer backbone, head that predicts the next patch — in value space.

The current time-series foundation-model landscape

Every recent TS foundation model forecasts in value space — only the head changes.

Model                                   Year    Backbone                                      Prediction target
TimesFM (Das et al.)                   2024    decoder-only transformer                     value (quantile)
Chronos (Ansari et al.)                2024    T5 enc-dec, tokenised values                 value (categorical)
MOIRAI (Woo et al.)                    2024    encoder, masked patches                       value (probabilistic)
Time-MoE (Shi et al.)                  2024    sparse MoE, ~1B params                       value (point + quantile)
Sundial (Liu et al.)                   2025    decoder + flow matching                      value (flow-based)
Timer-S1 (Liu et al.)                  2026    decoder-only, GIFT-Eval SOTA                value (quantile)

Why value-space prediction is structurally hard

- The observed series is one realisation of a stochastic process.
- A value-space loss penalises the model for missing the noise — noise no model can predict.
- Loss never reaches zero, even on processes whose predictable component is captured perfectly.

JEPA — predict a representation, not the values

Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA)
(I-JEPA (Assran et al. 2023); TS adaptations: LaT-PFN, TS-JEPA)

- context block
  - context encoder
    - context latent
      - predictor
        - predicted target latent

- target block
  - target encoder (EMA, stop-grad)
    - target latent

- Encode the context with one network and the target with another (EMA, stop-gradient).
- Train a predictor to map the context latent to the target latent — L² in latent space.
- Originated in vision (I-JEPA, Assran et al. 2023). TS adaptations: LaT-PFN, TS-JEPA.

JEPA alone is at risk of collapse

The constant function is a global minimum of the L² latent loss.
- If both encoders map every input to the same point c, the predictor matches it trivially.

Standing in the way: predictor asymmetry + EMA target + stop-gradient.
- Empirically robust, but no negative is ever explicitly enforced.

Contrastive learning gives us a structural remedy.

Contrastive learning

InfoNCE — explicit negatives stop the encoder collapsing
Used by CPC (Oord et al. 2018), T-Loss, TNC, TS2Vec

- InfoNCE (Oord et al. 2018): tractable lower bound on the mutual information between context and future.
- With explicit negatives, the constant function is one of the least stable solutions — collapse is actively repelled.
- Used in CPC, T-Loss, TNC, TS2Vec — but always for representation transfer, never as a TS-FM forecasting objective.

The unfilled cell

    no negatives                                      explicit negatives (InfoNCE)
value-space
target          TimesFM, Chronos,
                MOIRAI, Sundial,
                Timer-S1

                CPC, T-Loss, TNC,
                TS2Vec
                (representation transfer,
                not forecasting)

latent-space
target          I-JEPA,
                LaT-PFN, TS-JEPA

                Contrastive Forecasting
                (this work)

Latent-space target × explicit negatives — what we explore in this work.

Contrastive Forecasting

an encoder + a causal forecaster, trained jointly with a contrastive loss in latent space

Architecture at a glance

Contrastive Forecasting — joint training of encoder and forecaster

- Each (variable, patch) is encoded independently to a unit-norm latent h[t,c].
- Causal forecaster takes the past latents and emits a forecast latent f[t].

(Visual elements: x[t-2], x[t-1], x[t-0], encoder, h[t-2], h[t-1], h[t-0], causal forecaster, f[t], x[t+1], h[t+1], pull together (positive), + negatives (other series))

Patching and the unit sphere

Series of shape T x C x F → non-overlapping patches.
- Latent L²-normalised.
- Cosine similarity.

Encoder — the canonical residual block (TiDE / TimesFM)

Canonical residual block — TiDE (Das et al. 2023) and TimesFM (Das et al. 2024)
small MLP on the residual stream + linear skip + layer-norm

- Ubiquitous: TiDE (Das et al. 2023), TimesFM (Das et al. 2024).

Our encoder — bidirectional GRU on the W raw scalars

Our encoder — bidirectional GRU on the W raw scalars
modelled local temporal structure that an MLP collapses into a single linear projection

- Reads each patch as a sequence of W scalar timesteps; concatenates the forward and backward final hidden states.
  - GRU (fwd, 128)
  - GRU (bwd, 128)
  - concat (256)
  - Linear → H
  - Linear (skip)
  - +
  - LayerNorm → h ∈ ℝ^H

- Linear projection to H, residual skip from the raw patch, LayerNorm — same residual structure as the canonical block.

Forecaster — causal transformer

Forecaster — one transformer block
Pre-norm · depthwise conv → attention → FFN x4 · L = 12 · H = 1024
→ next block

    × L = 12

    +--------------------------------+
    | FFN x4 (GELU)                  |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------v----------------------+
    | LayerNorm                      |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------+----------------------+
    | +                            |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------v----------------------+
    | Causal Self-Attention         |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------v----------------------+
    | LayerNorm                      |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------v----------------------+
    | Depthwise                      |
    | causal conv (k=3)              |
    +--------+----------------------+
             |
    +--------v----------------------+
    | input                          |
    +--------------------------------+

    Elhage et al. 2021:
    short-range depthwise conv
    lets induction-head circuits
    form with one attention layer

• L = 12 layers, hidden width H = 1024
• Each layer: depthwise causal conv (k=3) → causal self-attention → FFN x4 (GELU), pre-norm.
• Depthwise conv on the residual stream — lets induction-head circuits form with one attention layer (Elhage et al. 2021), important in the small-model regime we work in.

Loss — contrastive in three flavours

Contrastive loss — three negative axes
L = −log P₊ / (N_ct + N_cc + N_cb)   τ = 0.07

- Cross-temporal: (h[t], h[t+1]) — same series, consecutive patches.
- Cross-channel: (h[t,c], h[t,c’]) — same series and time, different channels.
- Cross-batch: (h[t+1], f[t]) of different series.

Does the latent encode
the data-generating process?

We probe this on synthetic ARMA, where the generating coefficients are known.

Probing with synthetic ARMA

- Why synthetic? Ground-truth coefficients are known.
- Each sample: orders p, q ~ Uniform{1, ..., P}; coefficients ~ Uniform(−1, 1) or 𝒩(0, 1) (fair coin flip).
- Invertibility constraint: rescale so ∑|φᵢ| ≤ 0.95 and ∑|θᵢ| ≤ 0.95.
- ARMA(4,4), 8 coefs.
- T = 4096 timesteps per sample, patch width W = 32 → T_p = 128 patches.

Recovery head — a small probe on a frozen backbone

- Freeze the contrastively-trained backbone; fit a tiny head on top of the per-patch latents.

- Architecture: Linear ℝ^H → ℝ^128 → BiGRU(128, 2 layers) → 2-layer GELU MLP → two parallel linear heads (AR, MA) with tanh.

- ≈ 676 k parameters in total.

- Per-series prediction is the mean of the per-patch predictions: θ̂[b] = (1/T_p) Σ θ̂[b, t].

- MSE loss on the coefficients.

Recovery — what the latents tell us about the parameters

AR[0] r=0.915
AR[1] r=0.951
AR[2] r=0.958
AR[3] r=0.947

MA[0] r=0.917
MA[1] r=0.939
MA[2] r=0.955
MA[3] r=0.955

300 test samples · identity reference · Pearson r reported per panel.

Improvement over zero baseline (footnote): r = 6.96 (2x4) · r = 8.34 (2x2).

Open questions

- Real-world benchmarks — GIFT-Eval, Monash, ETT, electricity, traffic.
- Multivariate.
- Does f[t] encode the distribution of plausible h[t+1] futures?
- Scaling — beyond H = 1024 and L = 12; what does Contrastive Forecasting look like at 1 B parameters?
- Can the contrastive head replace value-space heads in TimesFM-class backbones?

# Contrastive InfoNCE loss

L(q, d+, D_N) = -log (exp(sim(q, d+)/τ) / Σ_{d_i ∈ {d+} ∪ D_N} exp(sim(q, d_i)/τ))

where q is the embedding of an anchor, d+ is the embedding of the positive item, and D_N denotes the set of negative items. sim(·) represents the cosine similarity function, and τ is the temperature hyperparameter.

TF-IDF

- Term Frequency: terms appearing more often in a document are more important for that document.
- Inverse Document Frequency: terms appearing in many documents are less useful
- Final score rewards documents that contain query terms often, especially if those terms are rare in the corpus.

Problems
- Repeated terms overboost
- Long documents have advantage

tf(t, d) = (f_d(t)) / (max_wed f_d(w))

idf(t, D) = ln(|D| / |{d ∈ D : t ∈ d}|)

tfidf(t, d, D) = tf(t, d) * idf(t, D)

tfidf'(t, d, D) = (idf(t, D) / |D|) + tfidf(t, d, D)

f_d(t) := frequency of term t in document d
D := corpus of documents

BM25

- BM25 scores documents by query term rarity, term frequency, and document length
- Intuition: BM25 is TF-IDF with term-frequency saturation and document-length normalization.

Parameters

Parameter    | Controls               | Typical Tuning Range | Intuition
-------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------
k1           | Term-frequency saturation | 0.5-2.5              | How much repeated terms help
b            | Length normalization    | 0-1.0                | long documents penalization factor

Where BM25 falls short

Query: car insurance
Relevant doc: "auto coverage policy"
→ BM25 misses synonyms.

Query: laptop battery dies quickly
Relevant doc: "device loses charge after"
→ Same meaning, different wording.

Query: GDPR deletion request
Relevant doc: "right to erasure under Article 17"
→ Domain terminology mismatch.

Query: how to cancel my account
Relevant doc: "subscription termination process"
→ User language vs internal language.

Latency & HNSW

- Hierarchical Navigable Small World
- Querying a vector database takes too long
- Because of the dimensionality common structures like M-Tree pruning stops working
- Only approximate (stuck in local minimum)
- Good recall latency trade off

HNSW Parameters

Parameter       | Stage          | Typical tuning range | Tradeoff
--------------- | -------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------------
M              | index build   | 8-64                 | graph degree, memory, recall ceiling
efConstruction | index build   | 50-500               | build time vs graph quality
efSearch       | query time    | 20-1000              | latency vs recall
dimensions     | index/query   | model-defined, often 256-3072 | quality vs memory and latency

Goal           | M      | efConstruction | efSearch
-------------- | ------ | -------------- | --------
Lower latency (baseline) | 4      | 400            | 100-500
Higher recall           | 6-8    | 600-800        | 700-1000
Max recall experiment   | 8-10   | 800-1000       | 1000

Hybrid Search

- BM25 catches exact lexical evidence
- Vector search catches semantic evidence
- Fusion turns multiple ranked lists into one candidate set
- Ranking decides the final order
- How are the sets merged when having different ranges
  - Cosine distance [-1, 1]
  - BM25 [0, x] where x >= 0

Reciprocal Rank Fusion

**[A] Standard RRF**
score(d) = Σ (1 / (k + rankᵢ(d)))
- Each retriever contributes equally.

**[B] Weighted RRF**
score(d) = Σ (wᵢ / (k + rankᵢ(d)))
- wᵢ = weight of retriever i
- Higher weight = more influence
- Uses ranks only, not raw scores

**[C] Two-set example**
BM25 results:
1 | D1
2 | D4
3 | D2

Vector results:
1 | D2
2 | D3
3 | D1

weights: BM25 = 0.7, Vector = 1.3 | k = 60
- D2 gets a strong fused score because it ranks in both lists and is #1 in Vector.

Fused top candidates: D2, D1, D3, D4

When to use
- Use weighting when one retriever is generally more reliable.

Common default
- Start with BM25 = 1.0 and Vector = 1.0

Tune
- Example: BM25 = 1.2, Vector = 0.8 when exact keyword match matters more.

Parameter k
- Typical default: k = 60

Metrics

- Recall@K: relevant docs are present
  - RAG candidate retrieval
- Precision@K: top K are relevant
  - user-facing search
- NDCG@K: graded relevance and order
  - Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain
  - ranked search quality
- MRR@K: how high is the first relevant result
  - known item lookup

- Latency@K: not a quality metric but should be taken into account
  - ensemble metric with those above

NDCG

1 **Assign relevance labels**
Each result is judged with a relevance label from 0 to 2.

```
Rank   Result   Rel (0–2)
1      Result A  2
2      Result B  1
3      Result C  2
4      Result D  0
...    ...      ...
```

Relevance legend (intuition)
2 = exact answer; should rank at the top
1 = somewhat relevant; acceptable lower in ranking
0 = not useful

2 **Map relevance to gain**
Relevance labels are converted into numeric gain.

gain(rel) = 2^rel − 1

Gain converts these usefulness judgments into numeric value.

Common mapping
rel | 0 | 1 | 2
gain(rel) | 0 | 1 | 3

Gain function can be customized to your needs.

3 **Compute discounted gain**
Discount gains by rank position. Earlier ranks matter more.

DCG@K = Σ (from i=1 to K) gain(rel_i) / log₂(i + 1)

Rank: 1  2  3  4  5 ...
Gain:  [high bar] [medium bar] [medium bar] [low bar] [low bar] ...

Higher ranks get less discount (position discount)

4 **Normalize by the ideal ranking**
NDCG is the DCG of the ideal ordering (sorted by rel. desc.).

NDCG@K = DCG@K of the ranking / IDCG@K

IDCG@K = DCG@K of the ideal ranking.

Range 0.0–1.0, higher is better

NDCG measures whether the most useful results appear near the top.

Search strategies
- grid search: few parameters, cheap eval
- random search: many parameters, uneven importance
- Bayesian optimization: expensive trials, 4+ meaningful parameters
- multi-objective search: quality and latency both matter

Narrow / sample next trials

Not every technical addition wins
- embeddings can reduce exact-match quality
- reranking can improve precision but miss latency budgets
- freshness boosts can bury evergreen runbooks
- popularity can reinforce already-visible documents

Overfitting

**Common failure**
Be careful about overfitting (overconfiguring)
- Many tunable parameters
  e.g., BM25 k1/b, vector topK, efSearch, RRF k, weights, boosts, reranker top-N...
- Many trials and tweaks
  trying lots of combinations and adjustments
- Repeatedly checking metrics
  peeking at results during development
- Result: offline gain that disappears after launch

**How overfitting happens**
Used for exploration
Used for selection
Used for final estimate
- Tuning queries → Validation queries → Holdout test queries
  Try many configurations
  Compare finalists
  Touch once at the end
- Principle: Separate exploration, selection, and final estimation. Don't optimize the benchmark—optimize the search experience.
- Only one final check

**Safer setup practices**
| Practice                     | Why it matters                                                                                     |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Train/dev/test split         | Keeps the final estimate honest                                                                     |
| Temporal split               | Avoids learning future behavior                                                                     |
| Head/torso/tail coverage     | Prevents optimizing only frequent queries                                                         |
| Deduplicate near-identical queries | Avoids leakage and inflated metrics                                                      |
| Freeze test set access       | Prevents metric shopping                                                                           |

**Same discipline as model training**
- Tuning (exploration) set
  Choose and tune search parameters
- Validation (selection) set
  Compare candidate configurations
- Holdout test set
  Final estimate, touched as little as possible

**Use temporal split to avoid learning future behavior**
- Older queries → More recent queries → Newest queries (protected)
  - Tuning queries: Explore many configurations
  - Validation queries: Select the best configuration
  - Holdout test queries: Final estimate only
- Do not let information flow from right to left

Search Observability (Production)

SIGNAL      SOURCE      WHAT IT CAN REVEAL
🔍 CTR      🔍 search bar      results look useful enough to click
📊 click position      🔍 search bar      ranking quality in the top results
🚫 zero-click rate      🔍 search bar      likely unsatisfied searches
🔄 reformulation within 30s      🔍 search bar or chatbot      user had to ask differently

⏱ dwell time / return rate      🔍 search bar      clicked result did or did not satisfy
📈 p50 / p95 / p99 latency by stage      🔍 both      where the budget is going

Azure AI Search

**[Concept]**
1. lexical retrieval
2. vector retrieval
3. hybrid retrieval
4. fusion
5. vector weighting
6. recency/popularity boosts

**[Azure AI Search / OpenAI capability]**
- full-text search scored with BM25
- vector fields with HNSW or exhaustive KNN
- text query plus "vectorQueries" in one request
- RRF for parallel result sets
- vector query weights
- scoring profiles with freshness / magnitude functions
- semantic ranker, reported separately as "@search.rerankerScore"
- Azure OpenAI / OpenAI "text-embedding-3-*" models

**[Replaceable pieces]**
- search engine
- embedding model
- reranker
- monitoring

These parts can be swapped while keeping the retrieval architecture pattern.

(1) Concrete Azure/OpenAI example; concepts remain portable.

Takeaway

- Search is not just embedding search
- Upon search performing badly you can try
  - BM25 and embeddings have complementary failure modes
  - RRF is a good starting point; labels let you tune
  - On wrong order of the search results add ranking

- Experiment on your own data; not every added signal improves search
- Guard against eval overfitting
- Keep the system measurable and replaceable

Responsibility of design

UX/UI creates a framework that enables users to truly leverage the potential of LLM tools while also providing relevant data for measurement.

We maximize UX when working with AI, which results in employees being satisfied with AI adoption. (our ENPS is +32 / ± 50% of employees truly supports AI).
